key_insight: Variable projection turns multi-snapshot spike deconvolution into a smaller nonconvex problem over spike locations, and the paper characterizes exactly when that objective becomes locally convex and well-behaved.
engineering_relevance: This gives practical guidance for choosing bandwidth, snapshot count, and initialization so sparse signal recovery can be made reliable in imaging, microscopy, and spectral estimation pipelines.
method: Eliminate amplitudes in closed form to form the VarProSD objective over locations only.
Characterize the basin of convexity using PSF power spectrum, smoothness, and spike separation.
Use Beurling–Selberg extremal approximations to bound conditioning and prove local convergence/error guarantees.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical experiments that validate the theory and show modified ESPRIT initialization followed by gradient refinement works well.
watch_item: The guarantees are local, so performance still depends on getting an initialization inside the basin of convexity.
paper_title: Characterization of the basin of convexity for multi-snapshot spike deconvolution via variable projection
